moment_text: Absolutely. So the journey really began in childhood. Honestly. I come from, you know, it's a little bit of a stereotype, but a long line of medical people as a Filipino American. Both of my grand-parents on my mom's side were physicians. Uh, my grandmother was an anesthesiologist, my grandfather was a surgeon, and they came over in a very interesting political time. Martial law had said in, in the Philippines, immigrated to Alabama from the Philippines. And this. Seventies and started life there. Not really expecting to stay and then they never went back. So I grew up hanging out with my family, Filipino get togethers with very busy doctor grandparents and that was just kind of the norm. And all of us grandkids, you know, grew up, you know, at the same time eating, take out Chinese and singing karaoke. We were all gonna be doctors, every single one of us. Again, a Filipino joke to go into medicine and I was really proud as a little kid too. Be the one who kind of stuck with it. You know, we have doctors in every generation, but I was the one in my generation who went all the way through. And so, honestly, I'll be honest, I, I didn't really think a lot about it when I was younger. I just knew I was gonna be a doctor and that was what I was gonna be little bit of that people pleaser disease, uh, good along there, but never considered anything else. I knew I wanted to be a doctor and probably the only other thing I knew I wanted to be was a mom and my career path kind. Brought the two together.
eligibility_rationale: This moment provides personal background and context but lacks the specific barrier, agency, or emotional signals required for insight eligibility.

moment_text: More to come on that later. Flash forward to medical school. I actually brought my grandmother on my med school interview. She was like my good luck charm. And she traveled with me to my interview where I ended up going, university of South Alabama. And as far as choosing O-B-G-Y-N, I kept hoping it would be something that chose me. I'll be honest, I was one of those like indecisive. I dunno what I want. I like everything. I like all the people. I like everything so much. And. I always tell students and residents my choice to be an ob. GYN was really kind of a non-decision. Uh, it was, it was good for a DHD adrenaline junkies, which is 100% me. The other piece was, it was so broad. I knew I liked talking to people. As you know, I'm a storyteller by nature. I knew I loved hearing people's stories throughout the generations. I loved the idea of getting to know people. Throughout their timeline, throughout their history, throughout having babies and going through menopause, I knew that there was the opportunity to grow within the field that I didn't have to narrow down too much. So O-B-G-Y-N is both surgical and office and delivering babies and up all night and er type emergencies and psych. You know, it's, it's a little bit of everything and I knew if I changed my mind, I could always do a fellowship and. Be an oncologist within women's health, or I could be a minimally invasive surgeon, or I could, you know, be a maternal fetal medicine doctor.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: has an agency signal (making_decisions) and a system interaction (medical_training).

moment_text: How I ended up in academic medicine before I left and you know, became a hospitalist where I am now. Was, uh, a bit of an accident as well. I had a baby in my third year of residency, totally planned. Thousands of miles away from home and I was Type A, I got into med school, I got married, I'm gonna have babies. This is the year I'm gonna do it. And it caught me a bit off guard. I had a baby in residency and all of a sudden that desired. To be a mother snuck up on me. I knew it was there, obviously. I knew it was there, but I didn't realize how hard it would be to do both. And it felt like I had to choose between my two loves my first love, which was my career, which was my entire life. As long as I can remember, I wanted to be a doctor, and then suddenly there's this baby annise and she suddenly became my everything. I, I tell her all the time, I, I don't think I knew what love was until I had you and. It really shocked me to the core, to the point that I actually walked out on my job my fourth year of residency, um, because it was so difficult. Breastfeeding, pumping. I didn't even think I would struggle with these things, you know? I, I didn't know what I didn't know. I didn't know these things were important to me and I didn't know the way out. And so I walked out on my job one day with plans to just be a stay-at-home mom and throw the whole career in medicine away.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: contains strong emotional signals (overwhelm, hopelessness), agency signals (withdrawing), and barrier signals (lack_of_support) within a medical training context.

moment_text: And then I had some great mentors. I had people who were like, don't quit you. We'll hire you. You're a great teacher. So the, the story of how I got into breastfeeding was really, I was a brand new mom with my first new job, and they said, well, you know, it's great that you join the faculty, but you gotta teach something. And I said, I don't know what to teach. I'm just obsessed with my baby. And somebody goes breastfeeding. I was like, oh, I could talk about that all day long because. As an OB, GYN, we weren't taught anything about breastfeeding or postpartum care. Nothing like, we just kind of, you dropped everybody, you know, we were pregnancy doctors and it became my career path to teach America's doctors how to take care of pregnant and postpartum women and how to take care of breastfeeding women. Um, and I became a mentor to my residents as well. So got my I-B-C-L-C, you know, I became. You know, the crunchy O-B-G-Y-N because I, I cared about the experience and, and really learned how much that meant to me when I didn't know that before.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: identifies a clear barrier (education_gap, training_gap) within medical training and demonstrates agency (making_decisions) in response to it.

moment_text: Yeah. Oh man. I had kind of a, a wake up call after having a baby, and I, I started to wonder would I have done this if it wasn't the default that I just thought I had to do this now Important piece of, you know, the family legacy is that my grandmother in the family, um, was also an opera singer. She had like a TV show in the Philippines and she performed while she was an anesthesiologist and she had six children, you know, as my therapist would say. Does that sound familiar? Yeah. Uh, I, I was determined not only to be a doctor, but to be a singer and to be, and to have a thousand children and to do it all. 'cause I saw my grandmother do it the way that I have done medicine is quite different from how a lot of people in O-B-G-Y-N have done medicine, and that's what has made it. My unique path, and I do feel like I have ownership of it, but it's because I stopped letting myself be defined by the rules that I thought went with the game. Let me tell you, there were a lot of rules and I was the ultimate rule follower. That's how I got so far. moment_text: So I went from undiagnosed postpartum depression that was not diagnosed until, you know, my baby was 10 months old. And that was a real light bulb moment. It's a a bit crazy making 'cause I'm like, wait a second, I diagnosed this. Like, how would I possibly miss this in myself? And, but it was such a relief, you know, it was such a relief to realize this was not who I am. This was something that was treatable and, you know, this cloud that I had over me was not, um, it didn't define the rest of my life.

moment_text: About 83% of moms initiate breastfeeding. So it's not a problem of trying to get people on board. Most of my patients want to do this. Like it's a no. Nobody even questions it. You know, some people may partially be breastfeeding, but you know, we see this giant gap from starting. To six months and you know, this has been kind of the healthy People 2020 goal, 2030 goal. It's really just trying to incrementally increase the number of people who are able to reach these milestones.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate by identifying a systemic barrier (lack_of_support) evidenced by a significant statistical gap in public health outcomes.

moment_text: And like I said, it's not because people don't want to, something's not working. Something's not working. And it is a systemic problem. Um, it's perceived insufficient milk supply, which is really, honestly, it's anxiety, it's food scarcity. It is the fear that drives you to do behaviors that actually could impact your milk supply. So it is a bit of a self-fulfilling prophecy.

moment_text: You have a fear and it's very natural, but it is so common that I'm gonna lose my milk. Some people overproduce because of this fear. Some people under produce, you know. 'cause I'm working 80 hours a week, I'm outsourcing everything and I can't even do this right. And I'm a freaking doctor and I can't do it right. moment_text: And with my second baby, I talked to a lactation consultant. I'm like, okay, here's the deal. Now I've graduated. What did I do wrong? Everybody gets a free helmet, we're gonna pay for your helmets, but without any direction, you know, and you gotta go cross country. eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the A2 evidence gate by detailing multiple significant systemic barriers, including 'system_navigation_difficulty' and 'cost_constraint', within the healthcare system.

moment_text: Oh, common question. Yes. So, you know, when it first started, it's a fairly new field for ob, GYN. Hospitalist medicine kind of started with the medicine field where they, they kind of split off. They're the office internal medicine doctors, and then they're the hospitals. Ones who only work in the hospital. They don't have an office. OB GYN started as. Laborists and then they evolve to hospitalists because we do more than labor and delivery. Um, and so the big difference between what I do now and what I used to do, which was kind of generalist, is that I don't have an office. I only work in the hospital. And at my every hospital's a little different. And the role for our hospitals might be different. Some places have academic roles. Where I work now, it it, my, my job every day is I assist on all the C-sections. So we always have two physicians doing C-sections. So there are a lot of other private practice groups. And it's really fun because I trained a lot of these doctors, so it's almost like I'm back in academia again. I get to still train them, um, and. I assist on difficult deliveries. Say they have twins and you know, they don't have backup who's comfortable. You know, I, I worked at some very high acuity hospitals, so it takes a lot to scare me.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is descriptive of a professional role and lacks barrier, agency, or significant patient-focused emotional signals, failing the A2 evidence gate.

moment_text: So I'm, I'm the backup, you know, and I think a lot of hospitalists are trained in emergency care and obstetrics, like we are the go-to. We keep it safe. We're the safety net people. We're there for emergencies. If somebody is not. In the hospital because they're in their office or they're taking a call from home and something comes in, which happens quite frequently. We're there. I, so this is why I spend the night at the hospital because. Sometimes it is very time sensitive, right? Like a uterine rupture or something. If somebody's not there in time to be able to diagnose this and take that patient back for surgery, I can ity split, get somebody back to surgery if I need to, 'cause I'm there. Um, and so you don't have like a traffic situation, a drive. It was very stressful actually for the private practice docs when these things, these rare occasions would happen. And there was nobody in the hospital to help take care of, you know, nobody designated to do that. So one of my primary roles in my job is to be the backup for the private practice docs.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate: Contains a barrier signal (fragmented_care) related to a healthcare_provider interaction.

moment_text: And then, you know, there are a lot of patients who come to the hospital, maybe they're visiting from out of town, maybe they were never able to establish care with an ob, GYN. Maybe they didn't know they were pregnant till 34 weeks and now they can't get in anywhere and they can't navigate the insurance system. So they, they will show up at whatever hospital has a labor and delivery, or they call the ambulance and the ambulance just takes them wherever. So these people are unassigned because they don't have a doctor at the hospital.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate: Contains barrier signals (access_barrier, system_navigation_difficulty) and an agency signal (seeking_care) related to a healthcare_provider interaction.

moment_text: So I will take care of any unassigned patients. Uh, maybe they have a doctor somewhere out of the city or somewhere else. And so it's pregnancy related things. So this is non-pregnant people, you know, sometimes it, it is early pregnancy. Ectopics. We do a lot of ectopic pregnancies. Um, sometimes it's a miscarriage where they're bleeding a lot and they haven't had time to establish care. So it's kind of a lot of people before they establish prenatal care where it's really early pregnancy care and they don't have a doctor, so I'll take care of those. Um, and then a lot of like. 40 something women with fibroids and perimenopause and horrible periods who are hemorrhaging, you know, that kind of thing. Sometimes it's sexually transmitted infections that have gotten really outta control, like to ovarian abscess, people who are really sick and septic from a, you know, a quick infection they didn't even know they had and it turns into something worse.

moment_text: Professionally, I speak out on it, you know, and it, it is difficult. So being able to comment on a national and, and, you know, state level Georgia OB GYN society and, and contribute in that way to how can we improve mental health in the postpartum period. For me, my box that I live in is the postpartum period. That's where I see a lot of the mental health issues. Um, and so advocating and supporting when people are trying to make changes, trying to screen and make sure, you know. I have the luxury of time since I don't have an office. You know when, when people show up in my emergency room to be able to really sit down with them and, and introduce them to resources that they may otherwise not know about, because. moment_text: So, you know, I speak out on my own platforms. Um, and then, you know, on the personal level, it again comes back to what you're doing here at Under the Sisterhood. When I tell them, you know, me too. I, I, I've been there. I mean, I was just recently diagnosed again and I had no idea and I'm like, oh my God, I've done it again. Like, how did I, how did I miss it again? But, you know, it, it really is kind of wild that that disease, there's so much we don't know about postpartum depression and depression in general, and normalizing that. Talking about it in my friend circles, my professional circles, and, and telling people my stories that, you know, I think. It's very easy to get a social media perspective of my life. And you know, I've even had friends who are like, oh, your life's so perfect. You have this like doula photographer for your baby and everything. And I'm like, I, I can't. I refuse to make that, that picture of my life because it's not accurate. You know, I have struggled. I'm on an antidepressant. I have no shame about that, man. moment_text: I think mine's more boots on the ground, uh, grassroots type of work with mental health. But for me, in the past, the most impactful thing has been telling them the story of my career. You know, every time I pitch for my company, it starts with the story of postpartum depression. And I can't tell you how many people come to me in tears after I pitch and they're like, I'm still crying 30 years later. You know, even dads, husbands, partners who are get very emotional about it and they're like. Where were you 10 years ago? We suffered so much, you know, and, and it really strikes a chord. So I think for me, tying it together on the entrepreneurial end and really kind of boosting companies that are working on maternal mental health as well has really, you know, kind of been a huge part of my career.

moment_text: I think probably.
